title: D1 Softball Manual Workbook Report
subtitle: April 2026 manual workbook import, translated into a publishable markdown brief.
intro_note: This report follows the notebook's editorial structure, but swaps in cleaner team-level angles for findings 02 and 07.
executive_summary: The imported workbook makes one thing clear immediately: the top end is crowded, but not random. Texas Tech owns the composite crown, UCLA and Oklahoma stay right on its heels, and the RPI table tells a different story with Arkansas at No. 1. At the player level, Megan Grant is the loudest bat in the file, while Tennessee's staff gives the cleanest prevention profile among innings-qualified teams.

storyboard.arc_title: A tight top tier with split ranking signals

steps.title: Top-5 race is primed for fan-facing weekly drama
steps.narrative: The top five teams sit within 0.492 composite points, which is tight enough for weekly movement to matter.
steps.transition: Start with the story fans feel first: the board is tight enough that weekly movement matters.

steps.title: Texas Tech leads the all-around team profile
steps.narrative: Texas Tech ranks first by composite score, signaling balanced strength across offense, pitching, discipline, and defense.
steps.transition: Show why Texas Tech sits at the top of the all-around leaderboard.

steps.title: Arkansas owns the RPI crown, but Texas Tech owns the composite crown
steps.narrative: RPI and the composite model are only moderately aligned here, with correlation at 0.40; Arkansas is No. 1 in RPI, but Texas Tech is No. 1 in composite.
steps.transition: Introduce the main contradiction: RPI crowns one team, the composite crowns another.

steps.title: Megan Grant leads the long-ball race
steps.narrative: Megan Grant is the loudest individual bat in the workbook, pairing a huge OPS with a home run total that sits far above the next tier.
steps.transition: Move from team shape to star power with the loudest bat in the workbook.

steps.title: Texas Tech profiles as a coaching-efficiency standout
steps.narrative: Texas Tech combines elite discipline with strong run-prevention markers, a profile that usually signals repeatable coaching value rather than one-off luck.
steps.transition: Translate the bat into a broader coaching and execution signal.

steps.title: Tennessee owns the strongest run-prevention staff
steps.narrative: Among innings-qualified staffs, Tennessee sits at the top of both ERA and WHIP, which gives it the cleanest prevention profile in the workbook.
steps.transition: Bring in the run-prevention staff that most clearly controls the game.

steps.title: Nebraska shows one of the cleanest balanced profiles
steps.narrative: Among innings-qualified teams, Nebraska keeps offense and pitching relatively close together, which usually travels better than a one-sided profile.
steps.transition: Add the balance note that explains why some teams hold their shape.

steps.title: The offensive leaderboard is top-heavy, but not singular
steps.narrative: Oklahoma, UCLA, Texas Tech, and Florida form the clearest offensive tier; the next few schools are good, but the gap to the top four is real.
steps.transition: Close with the offensive tier that sets the ceiling for the whole board.

deeper_analysis.question: Which teams overperform expected results once schedule strength is introduced?
deeper_analysis.importance: Separates true strength from context effects and improves ranking stability.

deeper_analysis.question: Which player profiles are most predictive of postseason run production?
deeper_analysis.importance: Identifies transferable offensive traits under stronger pitching.

deeper_analysis.question: Where does pitching usage create hidden fatigue or efficiency edges?
deeper_analysis.importance: Supports coaching decisions on rotation, bullpen leverage, and recovery windows.

deeper_analysis.question: Which fan-facing storylines have the highest week-to-week volatility?
deeper_analysis.importance: Improves content planning around credible, high-interest swings.

figures.title: Top 10 Teams by Composite Score
figures.caption: Texas Tech leads the composite board, with UCLA and Oklahoma close enough to keep the top tier live.

figures.title: RPI and Composite Rank Tell Different Top-10 Stories
figures.caption: RPI and the composite model only partially agree, which is why Arkansas and Texas Tech split the top-line story.
figures.alt: RPI and Composite Rank Tell Different Top-10 Stories

figures.title: Offense vs. Run Prevention
figures.caption: The best teams pair a real offense with enough run prevention to keep the run differential alive.

figures.title: Top 10 Players by OPS
figures.caption: Megan Grant and Jordan Woolery anchor the player leaderboard, with Katie Stewart and Emily LeGette in the next cluster.

figures.title: Tennessee Sets the Run-Prevention Standard
figures.caption: Tennessee owns the cleanest prevention profile, while Texas Tech and Alabama stay close enough to matter.

data_notes: The workbook is partial by design: batting and pitching rows are not fully overlapped, so player-level analysis is better treated as two complementary slices rather than a single joined table.
Team-level claims are strongest where both batting and pitching coverage exist, especially among the innings-qualified staffs used in the prevention charts.
The report uses the workbook as a snapshot, not a season-complete game-log feed.
Player batting and player pitching do not need to overlap for this workbook to be useful.
